cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.clinical_stem: 34-year-old presents confused, tachypnoeic, and febrile with ringing in the ears.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

expected_compensation.note: Low PaCO2 and low HCO3 are due to two primary processes: respiratory alkalosis and HAGMA

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the mixed respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis of salicylate toxicity
cases.explanation: Both PaCO2 and HCO3 are low. This is not explained by a single primary disorder alone. The low PaCO2 indicates a respiratory alkalosis, while the low HCO3 with raised anion gap (26.0) indicates a high anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is a mixed respiratory alkalosis and metabolic acidosis, classic for salicylate toxicity.

cases.title: Sepsis (respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 55-year-old presents to ED with with respiratory distress and hypotensive.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: Sepsis commonly causes respiratory alkalosis due to hyperventilation.

cases.title: DKA with vomiting (mixed metabolic disorder)
cases.clinical_stem: 24-year-old presents to ED with with abdominal pain and tachypnoeic and after missed insulin doses and dehydrated.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic disorder
questions_flow.prompt: Is an additional metabolic disorder present?

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 68-year-old taking loop diuretics presents with postural symptoms, dry mucous membranes, and fatigue after recent volume depletion.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~47.0; measured 46.6 is appropriate. Anion gap is 135 - (93 + 34) = 8, which is normal. This pattern is most consistent with diuretic-associated contraction alkalosis from diuretic use.

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 76-year-old attends ED with increased drowsiness on a background of known COPD.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: COPD exacerbations cause acute rises in CO2 on a background of chronic respiratory acidosis.

cases.title: Lactic acidosis (sepsis)
cases.clinical_stem: 60-year-old is admitted with hypotensive and febrile. Associated features include with increasing oxygen requirements.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.explanation: Sepsis commonly causes high anion gap metabolic acidosis due to lactate accumulation.

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: COPD exacerbations cause acute rises in CO2 on a background of chronic respiratory acidosis.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 24-year-old presents dehydrated and weak after several days of persistent upper gastrointestinal losses.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~49.8; measured 49.0 is appropriate, supporting metabolic alkalosis with expected respiratory compensation, as seen with vomiting.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 72-year-old with heart failure reports cramps and light-headedness after escalating loop diuretics for ankle swelling.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~46.3; measured 45.5 is appropriate. Anion gap is 139 - (93 + 33) = 13, which is normal. This pattern is most consistent with diuretic-associated contraction alkalosis from diuretic use.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 72-year-old with longstanding breathlessness presents with increased sputum and fatigue.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation
cases.explanation: pH is low or near-normal with elevated PaCO2, indicating respiratory acidosis. In chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by ~4 mmol/L per 10 mmHg PaCO2 above 40. Expected HCO3 is ~36.4; measured 35.6 is appropriately elevated, consistent with chronic respiratory compensation seen in COPD.

questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

expected_compensation.note: Low PaCO2 and low HCO3 are due to two primary processes: respiratory alkalosis and HAGMA

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the mixed respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis of salicylate toxicity
cases.explanation: Both PaCO2 and HCO3 are low. This is not explained by a single primary disorder alone. The low PaCO2 indicates a respiratory alkalosis, while the low HCO3 with raised anion gap (26.0) indicates a high anion gap metabolic acidosis. cases.title: Renal failure (uraemic HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 71-year-old with advanced CKD presents with malaise, reduced urine output, and progressive weakness over several days.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis from uraemia with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~26.0 (+/-2); measured 26.0 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 135 - (101 + 12) = 22, which is raised. This pattern is most consistent with renal failure causing uraemic high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

cases.title: Simple respiratory acidosis
cases.clinical_stem: 42-year-old is drowsy with mildly reduced respiratory effort after taking sedating medication.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~25.1; measured 25.4 is appropriate acute compensation. The anion gap is normal at 9.0. This pattern is consistent with acute hypoventilation.

cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~51.2; measured 51.2 is appropriate, supporting metabolic alkalosis with expected respiratory compensation, as seen with vomiting.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~21.5 (+/-2); measured 21.1 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 140 - (101 + 9) = 30 (raised), consistent with HAGMA such as DKA.

cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~27.5; measured 26.9 is appropriate acute compensation. This pattern fits acute hypoventilation, such as opioid toxicity.

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.clinical_stem: 34-year-old presents confused, tachypnoeic, and febrile with ringing in the ears.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

expected_compensation.note: Low PaCO2 and low HCO3 are due to two primary processes: respiratory alkalosis and HAGMA

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the mixed respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis of salicylate toxicity
cases.explanation: Both PaCO2 and HCO3 are low. This is not explained by a single primary disorder alone. The low PaCO2 indicates a respiratory alkalosis, while the low HCO3 with raised anion gap (20.0) indicates a high anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is a mixed respiratory alkalosis and metabolic acidosis, classic for salicylate toxicity.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 65-year-old with chronic respiratory symptoms presents with worsening shortness of breath over several days.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation
cases.explanation: pH is low or near-normal with elevated PaCO2, indicating respiratory acidosis. In chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by ~4 mmol/L per 10 mmHg PaCO2 above 40. Expected HCO3 is ~34.8; measured 34.1 is appropriately elevated, consistent with chronic respiratory compensation seen in COPD.

cases.explanation: Both PaCO2 and HCO3 are low. This is not explained by a single primary disorder alone. The low PaCO2 indicates a respiratory alkalosis, while the low HCO3 with raised anion gap (28.0) indicates a high anion gap metabolic acidosis. cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~25.4; measured 24.9 is appropriate acute compensation. The anion gap is normal at 12.0. This pattern is consistent with acute hypoventilation.

cases.title: Panic / hyperventilation (acute respiratory alkalosis)

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. In acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~22.4; measured 22.1 is appropriate, consistent with acute hyperventilation such as panic.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. For acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~20.8; measured 21.0 is appropriate acute compensation. The anion gap is normal at 11.0. This pattern is consistent with simple hyperventilation.

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)

cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 34-year-old presents dehydrated and weak after several days of high-output gastrointestinal losses.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: The pH is in the normal range, but the low HCO3 still indicates a primary metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~27.5 (+/-2); measured 29.4 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 141 - (116 + 13) = 12, which is normal, consistent with NAGMA such as diarrhoea.

cases.title: Renal failure (uraemic HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 71-year-old with advanced CKD presents with malaise, reduced urine output, and progressive weakness over several days.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis from uraemia with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~27.5 (+/-2); measured 27.9 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 134 - (96 + 13) = 25, which is raised. This pattern is most consistent with renal failure causing uraemic high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~32.0 (+/-2); measured 32.4 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 136 - (100 + 16) = 20, which is raised. This pattern is most consistent with renal failure causing uraemic high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 81-year-old on regular frusemide presents with dizziness, weakness, and poor oral intake after several days of increased diuretic use.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~47.7; measured 46.9 is appropriate. Anion gap is 140 - (95 + 35) = 10, which is normal. This pattern is most consistent with diuretic-associated contraction alkalosis from diuretic use.

Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~36.5 (+/-2); measured 36.4 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 140 - (113 + 19) = 8, which is normal, consistent with NAGMA such as diarrhoea.
